question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the missing validation of the GrpcProxyEndpoint field in nodeEntry (parsed but never used in the network map) to inject a crafted grpc_proxy_endpoint that, if the SDK is updated to use it, would route transactions through a fee-manipulating proxy?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() does not enforce a maximum number of nodes in the network map passed to hiero.ClientForNetwork(netmap), allowing a crafted mirror REST response with thousands of fake node entries to cause the SDK to select from a pool dominated by attacker-controlled fee-inflating nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client created by newClient() uses the SDK's default transaction fee behavior, which may include automatic fee bumping on retry, causing the effective fee paid per successful transaction to exceed the design parameter when multiple retries are needed?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the missing validation that cfg.operatorID corresponds to a real funded account before calling client.SetOperator(), causing the hiero.Client to be initialized with an invalid operator that results in fee-related transaction failures that could be exploited to manipulate the pinger's behavior?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() does not set any fee-related configuration on the hiero.Client after calling client.SetOperator(opID, opKey), leaving all fee parameters at SDK defaults, so that the effective max fee is determined entirely by the SDK library version rather than any application-defined design parameter?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client returned by newClient() has no mechanism to detect or reject transactions where the charged fee exceeds a design-specified threshold, allowing any fee amount up to the SDK default to be silently accepted and paid from the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the missing timeout on the hiero.Client's gRPC connections (only the HTTP client for mirror REST has a timeout via cfg.mirrorNodeClientTimeout) to cause the SDK to hang indefinitely on fee negotiation with a slow malicious node, blocking the pinger and causing it to miss fee-related design constraints?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() does not validate that the network map returned by buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() contains only legitimate consensus node account IDs (e.g., 0.0.3 through 0.0.N), allowing injection of arbitrary account IDs that the SDK may treat as valid nodes with non-standard fee behavior?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client created by newClient() does not implement any fee anomaly detection, so that a sudden spike in charged fees (e.g., due to a fee schedule change or node manipulation) goes undetected and the operator account is drained without any alert or circuit breaker?
